title: pac4j-jwt CVSS 10.0 Authentication Bypass via Forged Encrypted JWT Tokens: Null Check Bug Allows Attackers with RSA Public Key to Impersonate Any User Including Administrators in Java Enterprise Applications (CVE-2026-29000)
affected_products: Java web applications using pac4j JwtAuthenticator

sources.name: CodeAnt AI Security Research - CVE-2026-29000: Critical Authentication Bypass in pac4j-jwt
CyberPress - Critical pac4j-jwt Authentication Bypass Lets Attackers Impersonate Any User
TheHackerWire - pac4j-jwt Authentication Bypass via Forged Encrypted JWTs
FieldEffect - Researchers report a maximum-severity flaw in a Java JWT library
CIS Security Advisory - A Vulnerability in pac4j-jwt (JwtAuthenticator) Could Allow for Authentication Bypass
UV Cyber - Threat Advisory: Pac4j-JWT Vulnerability
OffSeq Threat Radar - CVE-2026-29000 CWE-347 Improper Verification of Cryptographic Signature

timeline.event: CodeAnt AI Security Research privately disclosed CVE-2026-29000 to the pac4j project maintainers
pac4j project released patched versions: 4.5.9, 5.7.9, and 6.3.3 for all supported major version lines
Public disclosure of CVE-2026-29000 with CVSS 10.0 Critical rating; CodeAnt AI published full technical analysis; NVD and GitHub Advisory Database entries published
CIS, FieldEffect, UV Cyber, and multiple security news outlets published advisories and coverage; Tenable added CVE to its database
No confirmed active exploitation in the wild as of briefing date; proof-of-concept code publicly available from CodeAnt AI research

simple_summary.what_happened: Researchers discovered a critical flaw (CVSS 10.0) in pac4j-jwt, a widely-used Java security library that many enterprise applications rely on to verify login tokens (JWTs). The bug is a single null check: when an attacker sends a specially crafted encrypted token, the library skips the step that normally validates whether the token's digital signature is real. Because of this oversight, anyone who knows the server's public RSA key — which is publicly available by design — can forge a token claiming to be any user, including a system administrator. The fix was released on March 2, 2026, but all deployments running older versions remain vulnerable.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Any Java-based web application, microservice, or API that uses the pac4j-jwt library (org.pac4j:pac4j-jwt) for authentication, specifically versions prior to 4.5.9, 5.7.9, or 6.3.3. Affected deployments include Spring Boot applications, REST APIs, single sign-on (SSO) providers, OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect implementations, and microservice architectures. The pac4j library is used across thousands of enterprise Java applications worldwide.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: The attacker identifies a target application that uses pac4j-jwt for authentication — this can be determined by examining application behavior, error messages, or public API documentation. Step 2: The attacker obtains the server's RSA public key, which is typically exposed at the standard JWKS (JSON Web Key Set) endpoint (e.g., /.well-known/jwks.json) or discoverable through API enumeration. Step 3: The attacker creates an unsigned PlainJWT token containing arbitrary identity claims — for example, claiming to be the administrator account with full permissions — and wraps it inside a JWE (JSON Web Encryption) envelope using the server's RSA public key. Step 4: The attacker submits this forged encrypted token to the application's authentication endpoint; the server decrypts the outer JWE layer correctly, but due to the null check bug, skips signature verification on the unsigned inner token and accepts it as valid. Step 5: The attacker now has a valid session as any user they chose to impersonate — including the highest-privilege administrator — and can access all application data, modify configurations, exfiltrate sensitive information, and pivot to other connected systems.
what_you_should_do: Audit all Java applications in your environment for pac4j-jwt dependencies using dependency scanning tools (e.g., mvn dependency:tree, Snyk, Dependabot) — check for org.pac4j:pac4j-jwt versions below 4.5.9, 5.7.9, or 6.3.3
Upgrade affected pac4j-jwt dependencies to the patched versions: 4.5.9+ for the 4.x line, 5.7.9+ for the 5.x line, and 6.3.3+ for the 6.x line
If immediate patching is not possible, disable or restrict external access to authentication endpoints using pac4j-jwt and implement additional network-level access controls
Review authentication and access logs for anomalies: look for new admin-level accounts appearing unexpectedly, successful authentications from unusual IP addresses, and privilege escalation events
Consider whether your application exposes JWKS endpoints or public key material that could assist attackers in crafting forged tokens
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: Authentication bypass vulnerabilities in security libraries are among the most dangerous categories of flaws because they undermine the entire premise that a system knows who it is talking to. A successful exploit of CVE-2026-29000 requires nothing but network access and the server's public key — information that is deliberately public. An attacker who exploits this becomes any user they want, instantly and silently. Because pac4j is used across thousands of enterprise Java applications — from REST APIs to SSO providers — a single unpatched instance can expose entire organizations to data theft, account takeover, and lateral movement across connected services.

tactics.description: Attackers exploit the authentication bypass in pac4j-jwt's JwtAuthenticator to gain unauthorized access to internet-facing Java web applications and APIs without valid credentials
Attackers forge JWT authentication tokens using the target server's RSA public key, effectively generating valid application access tokens for any identity including administrator accounts
By forging JWT claims with arbitrary role and permission assertions, attackers immediately obtain administrator-level privileges without requiring actual credential compromise
Forged tokens appear as legitimate authenticated sessions to the application, making exploitation extremely difficult to distinguish from normal user activity in standard access logs

tactics.technique_name: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Application Access Token
tactics.description: Forged pac4j JWT tokens may be reused or adapted to access other microservices, downstream APIs, or connected systems in architectures where JWT claims are passed between services without re-validation

detection_guidance.summary: Detection focuses on identifying anomalous JWT authentication patterns — specifically PlainJWT tokens wrapped in JWE containers, new administrator sessions from unexpected IP addresses, and sudden privilege escalations in application logs. Dependency scanning to identify vulnerable pac4j-jwt versions in production is the highest-priority immediate detection action.

log_sources.description: Monitor for successful authentication events from IP addresses not seen before for a given account, sudden admin-level logins by accounts with no prior activity history, and sessions with unusually broad permission claims

log_sources.source: Java Application Framework Logs (Spring Boot / pac4j debug logs)
log_sources.description: If pac4j debug logging is enabled, look for JWE decryption events where the inner token type is PlainJWT (unsigned), or signature verification skip events — these indicate exploitation in progress
Monitor Authorization headers and JWT tokens in requests for structural anomalies: compact JWE tokens (5 Base64url segments) being submitted where only JWS tokens (3 segments) were expected
Correlate successful logins with role=admin or high-privilege claims from new or unusual source IPs, especially where no prior authentication history exists for that account-IP combination
Scan the software bill of materials (SBOM) or Maven dependency tree of all Java applications for org.pac4j:pac4j-jwt versions < 4.5.9, < 5.7.9, or < 6.3.3

what_to_look_for.indicator: JWT tokens with JWE structure (5-part compact format) submitting PlainJWT inner tokens
what_to_look_for.description: A forged exploit token has a 5-segment Base64url structure (JWE) when decoded reveals an unsigned PlainJWT (only header and payload, no signature segment). Legitimate signed JWTs have a 3-segment structure.

what_to_look_for.indicator: Sudden appearance of admin sessions from new IP addresses
what_to_look_for.description: New administrator or high-privilege sessions originating from IP addresses with no prior authentication history for that account may indicate successful exploitation via forged admin JWT claims
what_to_look_for.log_field: auth.success=true AND auth.role=admin AND src_ip NOT IN (known_admin_ips)

what_to_look_for.indicator: Successful authentication events without corresponding password validation
what_to_look_for.description: In systems where JWT and password auth coexist, a successful JWT-based authentication that has no corresponding user record in the identity store may indicate a forged claim for a non-existent user

what_to_look_for.indicator: JWKS endpoint access followed by authentication requests
what_to_look_for.description: A sequence of access to /.well-known/jwks.json or equivalent public key endpoint from an IP address followed shortly by authentication attempts from that same IP may indicate active exploitation preparation
what_to_look_for.log_field: http.url CONTAINS '.well-known/jwks.json' AND src_ip EXISTS IN (recent_auth_attempts.src_ip)

immediate_actions.action: Scan all Java applications for vulnerable pac4j-jwt versions
immediate_actions.description: Run dependency scanning across all Java applications to find any using org.pac4j:pac4j-jwt versions below 4.5.9, 5.7.9, or 6.3.3. Focus on externally-accessible applications first.
Update pom.xml or build.gradle files to reference patched pac4j-jwt versions: 4.5.9+ for 4.x, 5.7.9+ for 5.x, 6.3.3+ for 6.x. Rebuild and redeploy all affected applications.

immediate_actions.action: Restrict access to JWKS and public key endpoints
immediate_actions.description: Review whether your applications expose RSA public keys via JWKS endpoints and consider whether public access is necessary. While JWKS is typically public, reducing unnecessary exposure limits attacker reconnaissance.

immediate_actions.action: Enable strict JWT algorithm enforcement in pac4j configuration
immediate_actions.description: In pac4j JwtAuthenticator configuration, explicitly configure an allowlist of accepted JWT algorithms (RS256, ES256) and disable acceptance of JWE-only or 'none' algorithm tokens to add defense-in-depth even before patching.

immediate_actions.action: Audit recent authentication logs for exploitation indicators
immediate_actions.description: Review the last 7-30 days of authentication logs for suspicious admin sessions, new accounts with unexpected privileges, or successful logins from unusual geographic locations or IP ranges. Treat any confirmed anomalies as potential compromise.
